title: To G. M. W. And G. F. W.
content:
I

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whenas, (I love that ן¿½whenasן¿½ word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It shows I am a poet, too,)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Q. Horace Flaccus gaily stirred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The welkin with his tra-la-loo,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He little thought one donkeyן¿½s back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would carry thus a double load,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Father and son upon one jack,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Galumphing down the Tibur Road.

II

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old is the tale, Aesopן¿½s, I think,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of that famed miller and his son
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose fortunes were so ן¿½on the blinkן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They had one donk, and only one;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know the tale, the criticן¿½s squawk
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (As pater that poor ass bestrode),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Selfish! To make thy fine son walk!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps that was on Tibur Road?

III

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You will recall how dad got down
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And made the son the ass bestride:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The critics shouted with a frown:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Shame, boy! pray let thy father ride!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Up got the dad beside the son;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The donkey staggered with the load
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Poor donk! For shame!ן¿½ cried every one
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That walked the (was it?) Tibur Road.

IV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know the end! Upon their backs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Daddy and son with much ado
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boosted that most surprised of jacks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He kicked, and off the bridge he flew;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He! haw!ן¿½ A splash! A gurgling sound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A long, last watery abode,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Anioן¿½s stream the donk was drowned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (If this occurred on Tibur Road.)

V

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let Donkey represent the Odes;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Miller represent G. M.;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Son stand for G. F.; the loads
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Critics, I will do for them.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, then, this proposition made,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And my bum verses ן¿½Ahן¿½dן¿½ and ן¿½Ohן¿½d!ן¿½).
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What Q. E. D. can be displayed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anent this ן¿½On the Tibur Roadן¿½?

VI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ First, Horryן¿½s dead and he donן¿½t care,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So cancel him, and let him snore;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His Donkey has been raised in air
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So oft heן¿½s tough and calloused oן¿½er;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our Miller, dusty-headed man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Follows the best donk-boosting code:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our Son, dispute it no one can,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sings gaily down the Tibur Road.

VII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This, then, must be this Criticן¿½s scream:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The donk was boosted well and high,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, ergo! falling in the stream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Isnן¿½t and ainן¿½t and canן¿½t be dry;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor is your book. Which is to say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is no gloomy episode,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½ve made a dead donk sweetly bray,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And joyful is the Tibur Road.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Ellis Parker Butler, titled "To G. M. W. And G. F. W.", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
